author: Ghulam Muhammad, Gulshan, Miyan
title: Bengal ka Jadu maruf Barbadi wa Abadi
gloss: A domestic novel; the hero enters the Indian Civil Service.
notes: 'The Magic of Bengal, known as Ruin and Prosperity' — ICS-career domestic novel.

author: Harnam Singh, Bhai
title: Mahan Bir Singh
gloss: The story of the boy hero Kundan Lal, who by the help of his Sikh friends, twice rescued his beloved Parbati from the hands of a powerful Muhammadan police officer and was afterwards married to her. The hero became a Sikh and adopted the name of Mahan Bir Singh.
notes: Conversion-to-Sikhism adventure plot with Muslim police officer as villain — communal boundary-drawing in popular fiction; compare Shudhi dialogue (p16 s15).

title: Jadugar
gloss: An adaptation of Reynold's novel entitled 'The Necromancer' by Munshi Dina Nath.
notes: G. W. M. Reynolds's The Necromancer adapted into Urdu — Victorian penny fiction entering the Punjabi market alongside Shakespeare (p19).

author: Ram Kishan, Hakim
title: Aina-i-Tamashbini
gloss: A novel shewing the evil effects of association with women of bad character such as prostitutes.
notes: Hakim Ram Kishan as author here; he publishes s30–31 (p19) — author/publisher/moralist in one figure.

author: Shadi Ram, Babu
title: Man Mohni
gloss: The story of a Hindu girl who passed the time of her widowhood with great patience and perseverance.
notes: Widowhood-endurance novel — counterpoint to the widow-remarriage advocacy verse at p13 s278; both positions in the same quarter's market.

author: Muhammad Husain, Azad, Maulvi, Shams-ul-Ulama
title: Darbar-i-Akbari
gloss: A history of the reign of Akbar, the great Mugal Emperor, with biographies of his chief ministers and nobles.
copyright: The Manager of the Azad Book Depôt, Lahore; No. 15—, dated the 25th December, 1910.
notes: Muhammad Husain Azad's Darbar-i-Akbari, 848 pp. quarto at 2,000 copies — the canonical Urdu prose monument on Akbar's court, second edition, copyrighted by the family book depot. Bracket note: previous edition noticed in entry No. 154 at page 34 of the Catalogue for the quarter ending the 31st March, 1899 — an 11-year edition gap the registry itself documents. Azad titled Shams-ul-Ulama in the entry.

flags.issue: copyright cell partially cut at page edge; register No. reads 159 or 160

title: Pocket History
gloss: An abridgment of Lethbridge's History of India in the form of questions and answers, compiled by M. Hukam Chand, a teacher in P. B. N., High School, Amritsar.
notes: '(Designed for educational purposes.)' printed above this entry. Lethbridge's imperial textbook reduced to Q&A cram format by a school teacher — colonial historiography at its most catechistic. No page count printed.
